extracted_information: THCA (Tetrahydrocannabinolic acid) is federally legal under the 2018 Farm Bill, provided it is derived from hemp and contains less than 0.3% delta-9 THC on a dry weight basis. This bill removed hemp and its derivatives from the federal Controlled Substances Act (CSA), making them more accessible. Although THCA is non-psychoactive in its raw form, it converts to psychoactive delta-9 THC when heated (decarboxylation). The Drug Enforcement Administration (DEA) considers this conversion, and products may be reclassified as marijuana (a Schedule I substance) if they exceed the 0.3% delta-9 THC limit after decarboxylation.

federal_THC_limit.basis: delta-9 THC on a dry weight
federal_THC_limit.note: Threshold for delta-9 THC content in industrial hemp and its derivatives, including THCA, to be federally legal and not a controlled substance.

features.name: Non-Psychoactive Nature
features.description: THCA itself does not produce the intoxicating or euphoric effects associated with THC.

features.name: Conversion to Delta-9 THC
features.description: Upon exposure to heat (e.g., smoking, vaporizing, baking), THCA undergoes decarboxylation, converting into delta-9 THC, which is the psychoactive compound.

federal_requirements: All hemp-derived THCA products must strictly adhere to the less than 0.3% delta-9 THC limit on a dry weight basis.

The DEA assesses products based on the potential conversion of THCA to delta-9 THC, post-decarboxylation. If the converted delta-9 THC content exceeds 0.3%, the product may be reclassified as marijuana under the Controlled Substances Act.
Testing laboratories must verify that THCA will not convert to delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol at or above 0.3% on a dry weight basis.
business_requirements: Businesses must comply with both federal and state regulations regarding testing and sales of THCA products.
Ongoing monitoring of evolving federal and state laws is crucial for sustained compliance.
Consultation with legal experts is recommended to navigate complex regulatory landscapes.
Consumer education about compliance obligations and legal risks is advised.

temporal_info.2018_farm_bill: Enacted in 2018, significantly altering the legal status of hemp and its derivatives.
